q: What's the difference between precision and recall when evaluating a retriever, and why can't you optimize just one?
a: Precision is the fraction of retrieved chunks that are actually relevant; recall is the fraction of all relevant chunks that you managed to retrieve. They trade off: returning more chunks raises recall but usually lowers precision, and a tiny top-k boosts precision but can miss context the generator needs. You pick the operating point based on whether the downstream LLM is hurt more by missing evidence (favor recall) or by distractor noise (favor precision).
fs: Frames it as a tunable trade-off tied to downstream generator behavior, not a single number to maximize.
fj: Says 'just maximize accuracy' or conflates precision and recall as the same retrieval-quality metric.

followups.q: Why is plain recall@k often misleading and what rank-aware metric do you prefer?
followups.a: Recall@k ignores where the relevant chunk landed, so a hit at position 10 counts the same as position 1 even though the generator weights early context more. Use a rank-aware metric like MRR or `nDCG@k` that rewards relevant chunks ranking high. This matters because rerankers and lost-in-the-middle effects make position, not just presence, decisive.

followups.q: A modern long-context LLM swallows 50 chunks fine. Does that kill the precision concern?
followups.a: No. Even when everything fits, distractors raise cost and latency and degrade quality via lost-in-the-middle and noise sensitivity, where the model latches onto an irrelevant-but-fluent passage. Bigger context windows shift the bottleneck from truncation to reasoning-over-noise, so precision still matters as a quality and cost lever, not just a fit constraint.

followups.q: How would you actually set top-k in practice rather than guessing?
followups.a: Sweep k on your golden set and plot end-to-end answer quality (faithfulness, correctness) against k, not retrieval recall in isolation. Quality usually rises then plateaus or dips as distractors creep in, giving you the operating point. Pin k where downstream quality peaks and add a reranker so you can keep k small without losing recall.

q: Define faithfulness in RAG evaluation and explain how a faithfulness score is actually computed.
a: Faithfulness measures whether the generated answer is grounded in the retrieved context, i.e. every claim it makes is supported by the passages, independent of whether the answer is also correct. The standard mechanism decomposes the answer into atomic claims, then uses an NLI model or LLM judge to label each claim as entailed, neutral, or contradicted by the context; the score is the fraction of claims that are entailed. A fluent, factually-true answer can still score low on faithfulness if its facts came from the model's parametric memory rather than the retrieved context.
fs: Separates faithfulness (grounded-in-context) from correctness, and names the claim-decomposition + NLI-entailment mechanism.
fj: Treats faithfulness as 'is the answer correct' and proposes a single embedding-similarity score.

followups.q: The claim-decomposition step itself uses an LLM. How does that bite you?
followups.a: Decomposition is a failure point: if it merges two assertions into one claim or drops a claim, the entailment check silently mis-scores. Over-splitting also inflates the denominator and dilutes a single hallucination. You validate the decomposer against human-segmented claims, and prefer atomic, independently-verifiable claims so each NLI label is clean.

followups.q: Why might you choose an NLI model over an LLM judge for the entailment step?
followups.a: A fine-tuned NLI model is cheaper, faster, and deterministic, which matters when you run faithfulness on every production response as a guardrail. It also resists prompt-injection and self-preference effects that bias an LLM judge. The trade-off is the NLI model is weaker on long, multi-hop, or paraphrase-heavy contexts where an LLM's reasoning wins.

followups.q: Can an answer be 100% faithful yet still useless? Give the case.
followups.a: Yes. If retrieval returned wrong-but-internally-consistent context, the answer faithfully grounds itself in bad evidence and scores a perfect 1.0 while being factually wrong. Faithfulness only measures answer-to-context grounding, not context-to-truth. That's why you pair it with context recall/precision upstream and a correctness check against ground truth.

q: In RAGAS, what do context precision and context recall measure, and what does each require as input?
a: Context precision measures whether the relevant retrieved chunks are ranked near the top (a rank-aware signal that the right context appears early), while context recall measures whether the retrieved context covers everything needed to produce the ground-truth answer. Context recall specifically needs a reference answer (or reference contexts) as ground truth, because it checks how many ground-truth claims are attributable to the retrieved context. Context precision can be computed reference-free by judging chunk relevance to the question, which makes recall the harder metric to run in production without labels.
fs: Knows context recall is the labeled, ground-truth-dependent metric and that precision is rank-aware.
fj: Describes both as generic 'how good is retrieval' with no mention of needing ground truth.

followups.q: If context recall needs labels, how do you monitor retrieval coverage in production?
followups.a: You can't compute true recall without references, so you proxy it: track context-precision and chunk-relevance online, sample live queries for offline labeling, and watch downstream faithfulness/correctness which drop when recall fails. Some teams synthesize references from a stronger model for a sampled slice. Recall is fundamentally an offline metric you approximate via correlated signals.

followups.q: Why is context precision rank-aware rather than a flat relevant/total ratio?
followups.a: Because the generator is sensitive to order; a relevant chunk buried at position 8 helps less than one at position 1. RAGAS context precision weights relevant chunks by their rank so a retriever that surfaces good chunks early scores higher. This directly evaluates whether your reranker is doing its job, not just whether relevant chunks exist somewhere in the set.

followups.q: How can context recall be high while the final answer is still wrong?
followups.a: Recall only confirms the needed evidence was retrieved; it says nothing about whether the generator used it. High recall plus low faithfulness localizes the bug to generation, not retrieval. This decomposition is the whole point of measuring components separately, so you don't waste time re-tuning the retriever when the model is ignoring good context.

q: What is an LLM-as-a-judge, and why is it so widely used despite being imperfect?
a: An LLM-as-a-judge uses a strong model, prompted with a rubric, to score or compare other models' outputs in place of (or to scale up) human raters. It's popular because it's far cheaper and faster than human annotation, handles open-ended outputs that exact-match can't grade, and correlates reasonably with human preference on many tasks. The catch is that it inherits its own biases and must be validated against human labels before you trust it as ground truth.
fs: Justifies it as a scalable proxy for human judgment that must itself be calibrated to humans.
fj: Treats the judge's score as objective truth and never mentions validating it against humans.

followups.q: What's the single biggest risk of letting an LLM judge close the loop on its own training data?
followups.a: Reward hacking and feedback collapse: if the judge scores outputs that then train or select the model, the system optimizes the judge's blind spots rather than real quality, drifting toward whatever the judge over-rewards (verbosity, confident tone). You break the loop by holding out a human gold set the judge never touches and periodically re-validating against it.

followups.q: When is an LLM judge the wrong tool entirely?
followups.a: When a cheaper deterministic check exists: exact-match for closed-form answers, schema validation for JSON, unit tests for code, or an NLI model for entailment. A judge adds cost, latency, and noise, so reserve it for genuinely open-ended quality (helpfulness, tone, reasoning) where no programmatic oracle exists.

followups.q: Does a stronger frontier judge always agree better with humans?
followups.a: Not necessarily. A more capable model can over-reason, apply its own preferences, or be more confidently biased, and on some subjective tasks a well-prompted mid-tier judge matches humans as well at a fraction of the cost. Judge choice is empirical: pick the model whose kappa against your humans is highest on your task, not the biggest one by default.

q: Name the main biases an LLM judge exhibits and one mitigation for each.
a: Position bias: the judge favors the first (or last) answer in a pairwise prompt, mitigated by running both orderings and averaging. Verbosity/length bias: it prefers longer, more fluent answers regardless of substance, mitigated by length normalization or explicit rubric penalties. Self-enhancement (self-preference) bias: it rates outputs from its own model family higher, mitigated by using a different judge model than the one under test and masking model identity. You can also reduce variance generally by giving few-shot calibrated examples in the rubric.
fs: Names position, verbosity, and self-preference bias with a concrete mitigation each.
fj: Says 'LLM judges are mostly fine' or only vaguely warns they 'can be biased.'

code.label: kill position bias
lines: # only count a win if it

followups.q: The both-orderings trick doubles judge cost. When isn't it worth it?
followups.a: For pointwise scoring there's no pair to swap, so position bias doesn't apply and you skip it. In high-volume online monitoring where you only need a coarse signal, sampling a fraction for the swapped check may suffice. You spend the double-call budget where decisions are consequential, like ship-gating pairwise comparisons or best-of-N selection.

followups.q: What's the failure mode when both orderings disagree?
followups.a: Disagreement means the judge can't reliably separate the two answers, which is itself signal: treat it as a tie rather than forcing a winner. Counting only double-win flips inflates ties but kills spurious position-driven wins. If too many pairs tie, your candidates are genuinely close or your rubric lacks discriminating power, and you tighten the rubric or add a margin requirement.

followups.q: Beyond the three classic ones, what bias bites RAG judges specifically?
followups.a: Sycophancy and authority bias: a judge over-trusts confident, citation-styled, or assertive answers even when the cited context doesn't support them. There's also a 'matches-the-reference-wording' bias that punishes valid paraphrases. Mitigate by grounding the judge in the actual retrieved context and rubric-scoring support rather than surface confidence or lexical overlap.

q: Your team uses GPT-4 to judge outputs from your GPT-4-based product. What specific risk does that create and how do you handle it?
a: Self-preference bias: a judge tends to rate text from its own model family higher, partly because it favors low-perplexity outputs that look familiar to it, so the judge is systematically biased toward your own system and inflates scores. The fix is to use a judge from a different model family (or an ensemble of judges), blind the judge to which system produced each output, and validate the judge's verdicts against a human-labeled gold set to measure the residual bias. If you must self-judge, at least report the gap between the judge's scores and human scores so the bias is visible.
fs: Names self-preference bias and proposes a cross-family judge plus human calibration.
fj: Sees no problem with a model grading its own family's outputs.

followups.q: Why does the low-perplexity explanation predict bias even when model identity is masked?
followups.a: Because the bias isn't about reading a name tag; the judge favors text whose token distribution matches its own generation tendencies, which masking can't hide. So blinding identity helps with explicit-attribution bias but not the deeper familiarity effect. The robust fix is a different-family judge whose perplexity landscape differs from the system under test.

followups.q: An ensemble of judges from one provider — does that fix self-preference?
followups.a: Only partly. Models from the same provider often share training data and tendencies, so the ensemble's biases correlate and the self-preference doesn't cancel. A useful ensemble spans distinct families (different vendors/architectures) so their errors are more independent. Otherwise you're averaging away variance but not the shared bias.

followups.q: How do you quantify the residual self-preference so you can report it?
followups.a: Score a held-out human-labeled set with both the same-family judge and a cross-family judge, then report each judge's bias as the signed gap from human labels per system. The difference in those gaps is your self-preference estimate. Tracking it over time catches inflation creeping back after a model upgrade silently realigns the judge with your product family.

q: What is a golden (eval) set and what properties make a good one?
a: A golden set is a curated, human-trusted collection of inputs with reference answers or labels that you treat as ground truth for gating and regression detection. A good one is representative of real traffic, covers hard and edge cases (not just the easy majority), is labeled with high inter-annotator agreement, and is version-controlled so results are comparable over time. It should be large enough for statistical power but small enough to label carefully, and held out from anything used to tune prompts or models.
fs: Calls out representativeness, edge-case coverage, labeling agreement, and versioning/holdout.
fj: Thinks a golden set is just 'some example inputs we tried once.'

followups.q: Why must the golden set be held out from prompt tuning, concretely?
followups.a: If you tune prompts against the same examples you gate on, you overfit to them: scores rise on the set but not on real traffic, and the gate stops catching regressions. Keep a separate dev set for iteration and reserve the golden set as an untouched gate, the same train/test discipline as ML. Otherwise the golden set silently becomes a training set.

followups.q: Your golden set goes stale as the product evolves. How do you maintain it without contaminating history?
followups.a: Version it: append new cases mined from real failures and tag a dataset version, never silently mutating old entries, so a score drop is attributable to the model not a changed set. Compare runs within a version. Periodically re-balance to current traffic intents while archiving retired cases for back-compat regression checks.

followups.q: How do you make sure edge cases don't get drowned out by the easy majority in the aggregate score?
followups.a: Stratify and report per-slice, not just one mean: bucket by intent, difficulty, and known failure modes, and gate on the weak slices independently. A weighted or worst-slice metric prevents a flood of easy cases from masking a regression on a rare-but-critical category. The aggregate is a headline; per-slice is where you catch real breakage.

q: How do you measure and improve inter-annotator agreement when building a labeled eval set, and why does it matter?
a: Use Cohen's kappa for two raters or Krippendorff's alpha when you have multiple raters with missing labels, and weighted kappa for ordinal/Likert scales so near-misses are penalized less than far ones; report the coefficient, not just raw percent agreement, because chance agreement inflates raw numbers. Low agreement means the rubric is ambiguous, so you tighten definitions, add positive and negative examples with explanations, and adjudicate disagreements before finalizing labels. It matters because your golden labels are the ceiling on any judge's measurable quality: if humans don't agree, no automated metric can be validated against them.
fs: Picks the right coefficient per scale type and treats low agreement as a rubric-design bug.
fj: Reports raw percent agreement and never connects it to rubric ambiguity.

followups.q: Why does raw percent agreement mislead, with a concrete number?
followups.a: On a binary label where 90% of cases are class A, two raters guessing 'A' every time agree 90% of the time by chance alone, yet have learned nothing. Kappa corrects for that expected agreement, so it would be near 0 here. Class imbalance is exactly when raw percent looks great and kappa exposes the emptiness.

followups.q: Why weighted (quadratic) kappa for a 1-5 Likert rather than plain kappa?
followups.a: Plain kappa treats a 4-vs-5 disagreement as just as bad as 1-vs-5, which is wrong for ordinal scales where adjacent scores are near-misses. Quadratic-weighted kappa penalizes disagreements by squared distance, rewarding raters who are close. It gives a fairer agreement picture and is the standard for graded quality scores.

followups.q: Agreement is stuck low even after rubric edits. What does that tell you about the task?
followups.a: It signals the task is genuinely subjective or the categories aren't separable, capping the achievable judge quality at that ceiling. Options: simplify to a coarser scale, split the ambiguous dimension into multiple clearer sub-questions, or accept the task is preference-based and switch to pairwise comparison where humans agree more readily than on absolute scores.

q: What is regression testing for an LLM application and how is it different from regression testing normal software?
a: It's re-running your eval set on every prompt, model, or retrieval change to catch quality drops before they ship, typically gating a merge in CI. It differs from normal software regression tests because outputs are non-deterministic and not exact-match, so you assert on metric thresholds (faithfulness, judge score, pass rate) and aggregate over many cases rather than checking one deterministic output. You also have to account for noise: a single flaky case shouldn't fail the build, so you compare distributions or set thresholds with margin.
fs: Frames it as threshold/distribution assertions in CI, accounting for non-determinism.
fj: Expects exact-string assertions like a unit test and fails on any output change.

followups.q: How do you stop noise from making the CI gate flaky without making it blind to real drops?
followups.a: Fix a seed and use deterministic decoding (`temperature=0`) where the product allows, run each case a few times and aggregate, and gate on a distribution or threshold-with-margin rather than any single case. Set the threshold from the baseline's observed variance so it tolerates noise but still fires on a genuine multi-case regression.

followups.q: Even at temperature 0 the same prompt can give different outputs across runs. Why, and what do you do?
followups.a: Greedy decoding isn't fully deterministic at the provider level: batching, hardware, and silent model updates introduce variation, and pinned IDs still drift. So you never assume bit-exact reproducibility; you assert on metric thresholds with margin and run multiple samples, treating the eval as statistical not exact even at temp 0.

followups.q: What do you do when a regression test fails but the new output is actually better than the reference?
followups.a: That's a stale-reference problem, common with reference-based exact/overlap metrics that punish valid improvements. You review the failure, and if the new output is genuinely better, update the golden reference (with versioning) rather than blocking the change. It's why teams prefer rubric/LLM-judge gates over brittle exact-match for open-ended outputs.

q: A prompt tweak improved your average judge score from 4.1 to 4.2 on 50 examples. Would you ship it?
a: Not on that evidence alone, because 0.1 on a 1-5 scale over only 50 noisy, judge-scored examples is almost certainly within noise. I'd compute a confidence interval or run a paired test (the same examples scored under both prompts, e.g. paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank) and bootstrap the difference to see if it's significant. I'd also slice by subpopulation to check the average isn't hiding a regression on an important segment, and confirm the gain is practically meaningful, not just statistically detectable.
fs: Demands a paired significance test and CI before trusting a small average delta.
fj: Ships immediately because 'the number went up.'

followups.q: Why paired Wilcoxon rather than an unpaired t-test here?
followups.a: Both prompts run on the same 50 items, so the scores are paired; a paired test removes per-item difficulty as a variance source and is far more powerful for the same N. Wilcoxon over t-test because judge scores on a 1-5 scale are ordinal and non-normal, so the rank-based test is safer than assuming Gaussian differences.

followups.q: Suppose the paired test does come back p=0.01. Any reason still not to ship?
followups.a: Yes: statistical significance isn't practical significance. A reliably-detected 0.1 gain may be too small to matter to users while the change adds latency or cost, and the average can still hide a regression on an important slice. Check effect size, guardrails, and per-slice results before shipping, then confirm on an online A/B against the real KPI.

followups.q: 50 examples feels small. How would you size it properly?
followups.a: Run a power analysis from the per-item score variance and the minimum effect you'd act on (MDE) to find N for ~80% power at alpha 0.05; noisy judge scores often need hundreds. Or reduce variance via the paired design and deterministic decoding so 50 carries more signal. Either way, decide N before the test, not after seeing the result.

q: How does SelfCheckGPT detect hallucinations without any reference or external knowledge?
a: It samples multiple stochastic completions for the same prompt and measures how consistent they are with the main answer, on the premise that genuinely-known facts stay stable across samples while hallucinated ones vary. Sentences that contradict or aren't supported by the other samples get a high hallucination score; the NLI-based variant (checking entailment between the answer and each sample) is the most effective and efficient of the implementations. It's reference-free, which makes it usable for online monitoring where you have no ground truth.
fs: Explains the consistency-across-samples premise and the NLI variant, and why it's reference-free.
fj: Thinks you always need a ground-truth source to detect hallucination.

followups.q: What kind of hallucination does SelfCheckGPT systematically miss?
followups.a: Confidently-wrong facts the model 'knows' consistently: if a memorized falsehood is stable across all samples, the consistency check sees agreement and passes it. SelfCheckGPT catches fabrication-driven variance, not systematic errors baked into the weights. For those you need an external knowledge source, which is the whole reason consistency methods complement rather than replace fact-checking.

followups.q: It requires several extra generations per answer. How does that constrain production use?
followups.a: Each sample multiplies cost and latency, so running full SelfCheckGPT inline on every request is usually too expensive. Teams sample a fraction of traffic, run it async out of the request path, or lower the sample count and accept noisier scores. It's better suited to monitoring and offline auditing than as a synchronous guardrail.

followups.q: Why does the NLI variant beat the n-gram and QA variants in the paper?
followups.a: N-gram consistency is fooled by paraphrase, and the QA variant adds an extra generation step with its own errors. NLI directly checks whether each sample entails the answer's claims, capturing semantic agreement regardless of wording, which aligns better with how humans judge consistency, at lower cost than the QA approach. That's why it's the recommended implementation.

q: Distinguish a faithfulness (grounding) failure from a factual hallucination, and why the distinction changes your detection strategy.
a: A grounding failure means the answer contradicts or isn't supported by the retrieved context, even if the claim happens to be true in the world; a factual hallucination means the claim is false against world knowledge regardless of context. For grounding you can detect cheaply with NLI between answer claims and the retrieved passages (no external knowledge needed), which is ideal for RAG. For world-factuality you need an external knowledge source or retrieval-based fact-checking (e.g. FActScore-style verification against a corpus), which is more expensive and is what you reach for when there's no trusted context to ground against.
fs: Separates 'unsupported by context' from 'false in the world' and maps each to the right detector.
fj: Uses 'hallucination' for everything and proposes one generic checker.

followups.q: In a RAG system, should you even care about world-factuality if grounding passes?
followups.a: Only if you can trust the context. If your corpus is authoritative, faithfulness-to-context is the right gate and world-checking is redundant. But if the corpus can be stale or wrong, a faithful answer can still mislead users, so you also need source-quality controls or fact-checking. The corpus's trustworthiness decides whether grounding alone is enough.

followups.q: Why is FActScore-style world fact-checking so much more expensive than NLI grounding?
followups.a: Grounding compares claims against context you already have in hand. World fact-checking must retrieve supporting evidence for each atomic claim from an external corpus first, then verify entailment, so every claim triggers its own retrieval plus judgment. That retrieval-per-claim fan-out is the cost driver, which is why you reserve it for when no trusted context exists.

followups.q: Can the two failure types co-occur and confuse your root-cause analysis?
followups.a: Yes: an answer can be both unsupported by context and false in the world, or faithful to wrong context. You disentangle them by checking grounding (answer-vs-context NLI) and world-truth (claim-vs-corpus) independently. Faithful-but-false points at bad retrieval/corpus; unfaithful-but-true points at the model ignoring context and using parametric memory.

q: Walk through the components you'd evaluate in a RAG pipeline and which metric isolates each.
a: Split it into retrieval and generation: context precision/recall isolate the retriever (did the right chunks come back and rank high), while faithfulness and answer relevancy isolate the generator (did it stay grounded and actually address the question). The trick is that a bad end-to-end answer could be either component's fault, so you measure them separately to localize the bug. For example, high context recall but low faithfulness points at the generator ignoring good context, whereas low context recall points at retrieval.
fs: Decomposes the pipeline and uses the metric pattern to localize which component failed.
fj: Only reports one end-to-end answer-quality number with no component attribution.

followups.q: Where does chunking/indexing fit — is it a retrieval or generation problem?
followups.a: It's upstream of retrieval and shows up as a recall ceiling: if chunks are too large, too small, or split a fact across boundaries, no retriever can return clean evidence. Diagnose it when context recall is low despite a good embedding model and reranker. Fixing chunk size, overlap, and boundaries often moves recall more than swapping the retriever.

followups.q: Low context precision but high faithfulness and correct answers — do you bother fixing retrieval?
followups.a: Maybe not for quality, but yes for cost and robustness. The generator is coping with noisy context now, but extra distractors raise tokens, latency, and noise-sensitivity risk as inputs get harder. Tightening precision via reranking is a cost/reliability win even when current quality is fine; weigh the engineering effort against the savings.

followups.q: Both retrieval and generation metrics look fine but users still complain. What's left to evaluate?
followups.a: The query-understanding and end-to-end layers your component metrics skip: query rewriting/routing, whether the question was answerable from the corpus at all, and answer correctness against ground truth. Component metrics on a stale golden set can all pass while production intents differ. Mine the complaints into the set and add an end-to-end correctness gate plus online monitoring.

q: Answer relevancy in RAGAS can be high even when the answer is wrong. Why, and what does that imply?
a: Answer relevancy measures how well the answer addresses the question (often by generating questions from the answer and comparing them to the original), not whether the answer is correct or grounded. So a confident, on-topic but factually wrong answer scores well on relevancy. The implication is you must never use relevancy alone as a quality gate; pair it with faithfulness for grounding and a correctness check against ground truth to catch the wrong-but-relevant case.
fs: Knows relevancy measures on-topic-ness, not correctness, and insists on pairing metrics.
fj: Assumes high relevancy means the answer is good.

followups.q: How does the generate-questions-from-the-answer mechanism actually score relevancy?
followups.a: RAGAS prompts an LLM to generate plausible questions the answer would address, embeds them, and measures cosine similarity to the original question; high similarity means the answer is on-topic. It also penalizes non-committal answers. Crucially it never touches ground truth, which is exactly why it can't detect a confidently wrong but on-topic response.

followups.q: What does a LOW answer relevancy score actually tell you to fix?
followups.a: Low relevancy flags incomplete, evasive, or off-topic answers, often because retrieval missed the needed context or the prompt let the model hedge. It points at question-answer alignment, not factual accuracy. So you investigate retrieval coverage and prompt instructions to be direct, distinct from a faithfulness drop which points at grounding.

followups.q: Why not just replace relevancy with answer correctness and drop it?
followups.a: Correctness needs labeled references, so it's offline-only, while relevancy is reference-free and runnable online. Relevancy also isolates the on-topic dimension independently of truth, helping localize evasive-answer bugs. They measure different things: keep relevancy for unlabeled monitoring and dimension isolation, and use correctness as the labeled truth gate offline.

q: How is evaluating an agent fundamentally different from evaluating a single LLM call?
a: An agent produces a multi-step trajectory of tool calls and reasoning, so you evaluate not just the final answer but the path: did it call the right tools with the right arguments, in a sensible order, without unnecessary or looping steps. That means three levels of metrics: end-to-end task success, trajectory quality (tool-call accuracy, step efficiency, progress rate vs the expected path), and component-level checks to pinpoint which tool or sub-agent broke. A correct final answer reached by a broken or wasteful trajectory is still a problem in production because of cost, latency, and fragility.
fs: Evaluates the trajectory and tool-calls at multiple levels, not just the final answer.
fj: Only checks whether the final answer is right, ignoring the path taken.

followups.q: Why is a correct answer via a wasteful trajectory actually a production problem?
followups.a: Each extra step multiplies cost and latency and adds a failure surface: more tool calls mean more chances for a timeout, rate limit, or bad argument. A loopy path that happens to converge today is fragile and will flake under load or on harder inputs. Step efficiency and trajectory quality are reliability and unit-economics metrics, not just elegance.

followups.q: How do you build a reference trajectory when there are several valid paths?
followups.a: You don't pin one path; you specify required tool calls (must-haves), forbidden ones, and constraints, then accept any trajectory satisfying them, often scored by an LLM judge for reasonableness. Exact-match only fits single-path tasks. For multi-path tasks, set-based or judge-based trajectory evaluation tolerates valid alternatives while still catching genuinely broken routes.

followups.q: Error compounds across steps in an agent. How does that change your metric design?
followups.a: A small per-step error rate explodes over many steps, so end-to-end success becomes rare and high-variance on long tasks. You add denser intermediate signals like progress rate and per-step tool accuracy to get usable gradient and lower-variance gates, and you localize failures to the first broken step. Relying only on a binary final flag wastes most of the run's signal.

q: How would you evaluate an agent's trajectory, and what's the difference between exact-match and LLM-judged trajectory evaluation?
a: You compare the agent's actual sequence of tool calls and arguments against a reference trajectory. Exact-match (or in-order/any-order set match) checks whether the called tools match the expected ones deterministically, which is strict and cheap but brittle when multiple valid paths exist. An LLM-judge trajectory eval instead reasons about whether the path was reasonable and goal-directed even if it differs from the reference, which tolerates valid alternative solutions but costs more and adds judge noise. Use exact-match when there's one correct tool sequence, and an LLM judge when many valid paths exist.
fs: Contrasts deterministic trajectory match vs LLM-judge and picks based on path uniqueness.
fj: Assumes there's exactly one correct trajectory and only string-matches tool names.

followups.q: The any-order subset check ignores arguments. Why is that a dangerous blind spot?
followups.a: Calling `search` with the wrong query or `refund` with the wrong amount passes a tool-name check while doing the wrong thing entirely. Arguments are where most real agent bugs live. You need argument-level matching (exact for IDs, semantic/judge for free-text params) layered on top of tool-presence, or the metric greenlights harmful calls.

followups.q: When does in-order matching matter and when does order-agnostic suffice?
followups.a: Order matters when steps have dependencies, e.g. you must `authenticate` before `transfer`, or fetch an ID before using it; out-of-order there is a real bug. Order-agnostic suffices when independent lookups can happen in any sequence. Pick the constraint from the task's actual dependency structure rather than defaulting to one.

followups.q: How do you keep the LLM trajectory judge from rewarding confident-but-wrong paths?
followups.a: Ground it in the actual tool outputs and a rubric of required sub-goals, not just the narrative, so it scores whether each step advanced a real sub-goal rather than whether the reasoning sounded plausible. Validate the trajectory judge against human-labeled trajectories with kappa, and probe it with deliberately broken-but-fluent paths to confirm it penalizes them.

q: Explain offline vs online evaluation for an LLM product and when each is used.
a: Offline evaluation runs a fixed, curated dataset with reference answers in a controlled setting, typically before deploy and in CI, so you can use reference-based metrics and catch regressions pre-merge. Online evaluation scores live production traffic in real time, where you have no ground truth, so you rely on reference-free signals (toxicity classifiers, faithfulness checks, user feedback, guardrails). They're complementary: offline catches known regressions before shipping, online surfaces drift, abuse, and distribution shift you didn't anticipate.
fs: Maps offline to reference-based pre-deploy gating and online to reference-free live monitoring.
fj: Treats them as interchangeable or thinks offline eval alone is sufficient.

followups.q: What online signals can act as proxies for the correctness you can't measure live?
followups.a: Implicit user behavior: thumbs up/down, copy/accept rates, follow-up rephrasings, conversation abandonment, and downstream conversion. Plus reference-free model checks like faithfulness and toxicity. None is ground truth, but together they correlate with quality and flag regressions, and you sample a slice for offline human labeling to calibrate them.

followups.q: How do you close the loop from online findings back to offline?
followups.a: Mine production failures, low-feedback turns, and new intents, label them, and append them to the versioned golden set so the next offline gate covers what online surfaced. This stops the offline set from going stale and turns each production surprise into a permanent regression test. The loop is what keeps offline eval representative.

followups.q: If offline passes but online regresses, where do you look first?
followups.a: Distribution shift: the golden set didn't represent the cohort that broke, so the offline gain was real on a narrow slice. Check whether real intents differ from your set, whether a guardrail like latency regressed, and whether the change overfit the eval examples. Then feed the failing cohort back into the offline set.

q: Why can a model that wins offline still lose in an online A/B test?
a: Offline metrics measure proxy quality on a static, possibly stale dataset, while the A/B test measures real user outcomes under live distribution, latency, and interaction effects your eval set never captured. Distribution shift (real prompts differ from your golden set), proxy-metric misalignment (judge score up but users don't convert), and operational costs like added latency can all flip the result. This is why you validate offline-online correlation and treat offline eval as a fast filter, not the final word; the A/B test on the real KPI is the arbiter.
fs: Cites distribution shift and proxy-vs-real-KPI gap, and trusts the online KPI as arbiter.
fj: Assumes a better offline score guarantees a better product.

followups.q: How would you actually measure offline-online correlation to know if your offline eval is trustworthy?
followups.a: Collect paired data points where you have both an offline metric and the realized online KPI across many shipped changes, then check rank correlation (Spearman) between offline delta and online delta. Strong correlation means offline is a useful filter; weak means your proxy is misaligned and you fix the metric or set before trusting it to gate.

followups.q: Give a concrete example of proxy-metric misalignment flipping the result.
followups.a: A prompt that makes answers longer and more thorough lifts an LLM judge's score (it likes detail) but users find the responses slower and harder to skim, so task-completion and retention drop in the A/B. The judge rewarded verbosity the real KPI penalizes. Length bias in the judge directly caused the offline-online divergence.

followups.q: If the A/B is the arbiter, why run offline eval at all?
followups.a: A/B tests are slow, expensive, and risky to expose to users, so you can't test every candidate live. Offline eval is the cheap fast filter that kills obvious regressions and narrows many candidates to a few worth A/B testing. It's a funnel: offline screens, online decides. Skipping offline floods the A/B pipeline with junk.

q: When A/B testing an LLM change, what are primary vs guardrail metrics and why do you need both?
a: The primary metric is the outcome you're trying to move (e.g. task completion or user retention), while guardrail metrics are things you must not regress (latency, cost per query, refusal rate, toxicity). You need both because an LLM change can lift the primary metric while quietly harming a guardrail; for example a more verbose, helpful answer might raise satisfaction but blow up latency and token cost. You only roll out if the treatment wins on the primary metric without violating any guardrail threshold.
fs: Pairs a primary KPI with explicit guardrails and gates rollout on not regressing them.
fj: Tracks only the headline metric and ignores cost/latency/safety side effects.

followups.q: Testing many guardrails at once inflates false positives. How do you handle the multiple-comparisons problem?
followups.a: Each guardrail at alpha 0.05 means the chance one fires spuriously grows with the count. Use a correction (Bonferroni/Benjamini-Hochberg) or set guardrails as non-inferiority bounds with a tolerance margin rather than strict equality tests, so small noise doesn't block ships. Reserve full significance power for the primary metric; treat guardrails as ceilings, not hypotheses to hunt.

followups.q: Why frame a guardrail as non-inferiority rather than a plain significance test?
followups.a: You don't need to prove latency got better, only that it didn't get meaningfully worse. A non-inferiority test asks whether the regression stays within an acceptable margin, which matches the real requirement and avoids blocking a good change over a tiny, harmless latency wobble. Strict equality tests are both underpowered and overly conservative for guardrails.

followups.q: Primary metric wins but one guardrail regresses slightly. How do you decide?
followups.a: It's a trade-off call against the guardrail's tolerance and the size of the primary win: a large completion lift may justify a few ms of latency, but any safety/toxicity regression is usually a hard no regardless of primary gains. Define which guardrails are hard ceilings versus soft budgets before the test so the decision isn't litigated after seeing data.

q: How do you decide sample size for an LLM A/B test, and why is it harder than a typical web experiment?
a: You run a power analysis from the baseline metric, the minimum effect you care about (MDE), and the metric's variance to find the sessions needed for, say, 80% power at alpha 0.05. It's harder for LLMs because outputs are stochastic and metrics like judge scores are noisier and often heavy-tailed, inflating variance and the required sample; under-powered tests fail to detect real wins. Reducing per-unit variance (deterministic decoding where possible, paired/within-subject designs, or variance-reduction like CUPED) lets you reach significance with fewer samples.
fs: Drives sample size from MDE and variance and notes LLM stochasticity inflates the requirement.
fj: Picks a round number of examples with no power analysis.

followups.q: How does CUPED cut required sample size without biasing the estimate?
followups.a: CUPED regresses out a pre-experiment covariate (the same unit's prior metric) that's correlated with the outcome but unaffected by treatment, removing that explained variance from the estimate. Less residual variance means a smaller N for the same power. It's unbiased because the covariate is measured before assignment, so it can't be moved by the treatment.

followups.q: Why do heavy-tailed judge scores specifically inflate N, and what's the fix?
followups.a: Heavy tails mean a few extreme scores dominate the variance, and the mean's standard error shrinks slowly, so you need many more samples to pin it down. Fixes: switch to a bounded, less-skewed metric like pass-rate over a threshold, winsorize outliers, or use a rank-based test. Reducing skew is often a bigger N win than collecting more data.

followups.q: Can you use within-subject paired design in a live user A/B, or only offline?
followups.a: Offline you freely run the same inputs through both variants (true pairing). Live, true within-subject is usually impossible because a user sees one variant, but you approximate it with CUPED using each user's pre-period behavior, or interleaving for ranking tasks. Pure paired designs are an offline luxury; online you lean on covariate-based variance reduction.

q: How do you design a good rubric for an LLM-as-a-judge, and should you use pointwise or pairwise scoring?
a: Make the rubric specific with discrete criteria, a constrained output format, and worked positive and negative examples with explanations, since concrete examples sharply raise agreement; vague 1-10 'quality' scales produce noisy, uncalibrated judgments. Pairwise (A vs B) is generally more reliable than pointwise because relative judgments are easier and more stable for both humans and judges, but it scales as O(n^2) comparisons and needs aggregation like Bradley-Terry/Elo to produce a ranking. Use pointwise with a clear rubric when you need an absolute threshold to gate on, and pairwise when you're comparing candidate systems.
fs: Builds a concrete example-driven rubric and chooses pairwise vs pointwise by the use case.
fj: Asks the judge to 'rate quality 1-10' with no criteria or examples.

followups.q: Pairwise is O(n^2). How do you rank many systems without all the comparisons?
followups.a: You don't run the full matrix; you sample comparisons and fit a Bradley-Terry or Elo model that infers a global ranking from sparse pairwise outcomes, like chess ratings. Active/adaptive sampling focuses comparisons on close pairs. That turns O(n^2) into a manageable budget while still producing a calibrated ordering.

followups.q: You need an absolute pass/fail gate but pairwise only gives relative order. What do you do?
followups.a: Pin an anchor: include a known reference answer of exactly threshold quality in the pairwise comparison, and pass any candidate that beats the anchor. That converts relative judgments into an absolute gate while keeping pairwise's reliability. Alternatively use a tightly-rubriced pointwise score validated against humans for the threshold.

followups.q: Why does asking the judge to explain before scoring (CoT) help, and when does it hurt?
followups.a: Requiring a rationale before the verdict forces the judge to check the rubric criteria, which raises agreement and reduces snap biases. It hurts when latency/cost matter (online guardrails) or when the rationale becomes post-hoc justification for a biased verdict. Constrain the rationale to per-criterion checks, and keep CoT for offline gating, lean scoring online.

q: Before you trust an LLM judge's scores, how do you validate the judge itself?
a: Treat the judge like any classifier: run it on a human-labeled gold set and measure agreement with the human labels (Cohen's/weighted kappa or correlation for ordinal scores), not just raw accuracy. Check it for the known biases by perturbing inputs (swap pairwise order, pad length, mask model identity) and confirm the verdict is stable. Only once agreement and bias are acceptable do you use it at scale, and you periodically re-validate because prompt or model upgrades can silently shift its behavior.
fs: Validates the judge against human gold with kappa/correlation and active bias probes.
fj: Assumes a frontier model is automatically a trustworthy judge with no validation.

followups.q: What's the perturbation-based bias probe in practice, concretely?
followups.a: You take labeled cases and apply a transformation that shouldn't change the verdict: swap A/B order, pad the correct answer with filler to test length bias, or relabel which model produced it. If the verdict flips, you've quantified that bias. It's a controlled stress test, distinct from just measuring overall agreement.

followups.q: Agreement is good overall but the judge is weak on one critical slice. Ship it?
followups.a: No, if that slice is decision-critical. Aggregate kappa can hide a judge that's near-random on a rare-but-important category (e.g. safety-sensitive cases). Validate and gate per-slice, and either fix the rubric for that slice or route those cases to humans. An aggregate number is necessary but not sufficient for trust.

followups.q: How often do you re-validate, and what triggers an immediate re-check?
followups.a: On a cadence and on any change to the judge model, judge prompt, or the rubric, since all three silently shift behavior, plus when provider model drift is detected. Log the judge model ID with every run for attribution. Treat the judge like production code: a change without re-validation is an untested deploy of your measurement instrument.

q: What's the difference between reference-based and reference-free evaluation, and when do you reach for each?
a: Reference-based evaluation compares an output to a known correct answer, so it needs labeled ground truth and powers metrics like exact-match, BLEU/ROUGE, or correctness checks. Reference-free evaluation scores an output on its own (or against the input/context) with no gold label, using signals like faithfulness, toxicity, or an LLM-judge rubric. You use reference-based offline where you can afford labels and want a clean pass/fail gate, and reference-free online where production traffic has no ground truth. Most real systems run both: reference-based pre-deploy, reference-free in monitoring.
fs: Ties reference-based to labeled offline gating and reference-free to unlabeled online monitoring.
fj: Thinks every eval needs a ground-truth answer, so online monitoring is impossible.

followups.q: Is faithfulness reference-based or reference-free, and why does that matter?
followups.a: It's reference-free in the labeled-answer sense: it checks the answer against the retrieved context, not a gold answer, so you can run it online with no labels. That's why it's a workhorse for production RAG monitoring. It's not reference-free of context, though, so it can't be used where there's no retrieved passage to ground against.

followups.q: What's the main weakness of reference-free LLM-judge scores versus reference-based metrics?
followups.a: Without ground truth the judge can be confidently wrong and there's nothing external to anchor it, so its biases go unchecked unless you validate it offline against humans. Reference-based metrics are objective given correct labels. The trade-off: reference-free scales to unlabeled live traffic but you must trust a calibrated judge rather than a fixed answer key.

followups.q: Give a case where reference-based exact-match is actively harmful.
followups.a: Open-ended generation: a valid paraphrase or a better-than-reference answer fails exact-match and gets marked wrong, punishing real improvements and blocking good changes. There, reference-free rubric judging or semantic similarity is right. Exact-match only fits closed-form outputs (a number, a label, a canonical string) where there's one correct surface form.

q: Why have BLEU and ROUGE fallen out of favor for evaluating LLM outputs, and what replaced them?
a: BLEU and ROUGE score n-gram overlap with a reference, so they punish valid paraphrases and synonyms and reward surface copying, which correlates poorly with human judgment on open-ended generation. Embedding-based metrics like BERTScore improve on this by matching contextual token embeddings instead of exact n-grams, lifting human alignment, but they still miss document-level coherence and can be fooled by fluent hallucinations. For most modern LLM tasks teams have moved to LLM-as-judge rubrics and task-specific checks (faithfulness, correctness against claims), keeping n-gram metrics only for narrow tasks like translation where references are tight.
fs: Explains n-gram brittleness to paraphrase and names the embedding/LLM-judge successors with their own limits.
fj: Still treats a high BLEU/ROUGE score as proof the answer is good.

followups.q: Where do BLEU and ROUGE still legitimately earn their keep?
followups.a: Tasks with tight, low-variance references: machine translation (BLEU), and extractive/headline summarization (ROUGE) where surface overlap genuinely tracks quality and references are constrained. They're also cheap, deterministic, and reproducible, useful as fast sanity signals or in research leaderboards. The brittleness only bites on open-ended generation with many valid phrasings.

followups.q: BERTScore fixes paraphrase but you said it's fooled by fluent hallucinations. Why?
followups.a: It measures embedding similarity to a reference, so a fluent answer that's semantically near the reference scores high even if it inverts a fact or adds a false claim, since embeddings capture topical similarity, not truth or claim-level support. It also can't see document-level coherence. That's why claim-level faithfulness/correctness checks superseded it.

followups.q: If LLM-judge rubrics are better, why not drop n-gram metrics from CI entirely?
followups.a: Cost, speed, and determinism: n-gram metrics run instantly and reproducibly with no API cost or judge noise, making them fine cheap guards for tight-reference tasks and useful as a fast pre-filter. You reserve the expensive LLM judge for open-ended quality. It's a tiered strategy, not strictly replace-everything.

q: What is eval-driven development and how does it change the way you build an LLM feature?
a: Eval-driven development applies TDD thinking to LLM apps: you write the eval set and metrics first, then iterate prompts, retrieval, or models until they pass, rather than eyeballing a few outputs. The golden set is created and version-controlled at the start from real traffic, edge cases, and known failure modes, and every change is judged against it. This flips development from 'looks good to me' to a measurable loop, so you can refactor prompts confidently and prove a change helped. The discipline also forces you to define what 'good' means before you start tuning.
fs: Frames evals-first as a measurable loop with a versioned golden set defined before tuning.
fj: Builds the prompt first and only adds evals later (or never), judging by vibes.

followups.q: Writing evals first for a brand-new feature with no traffic — how, with no data?
followups.a: You bootstrap: hand-author a small seed set from the product spec, expected intents, and anticipated failure modes, optionally synthesizing candidate cases with an LLM that a human curates. It's small and imperfect but defines 'good' before tuning. Then you grow and re-balance it with real traffic the moment the feature ships, treating v1 as a starting gate.

followups.q: What's the analog of overfitting in eval-driven development, and how do you avoid it?
followups.a: Tuning prompts directly against the gate set until scores rise on those exact cases but not on real traffic, prompt overfitting. Avoid it by iterating on a separate dev set and keeping the golden set as an untouched gate, and by refreshing the set from production. If golden scores climb but online KPIs don't, you've overfit the eval.

followups.q: Doesn't strict eval-first slow down early exploration?
followups.a: Heavyweight evals upfront can ossify a feature you're still shaping, so early on you keep the eval set lean and cheap and grow rigor as the feature stabilizes. The point isn't bureaucracy; it's having a measurable signal before you optimize. Match eval investment to feature maturity rather than front-loading a giant labeled set before product-market fit.

q: A 'better' prompt passed your offline eval but users complained after rollout. What likely went wrong?
a: Most likely your golden set didn't cover the production cohort that broke, so the offline gain was real on a stale, narrow slice but missed real-world distribution. This is eval-score drift: prompt rollouts can pass offline yet degrade on traffic the eval never sampled. The fix is to mine the complaints back into the golden set, stratify it by real user intents, and add online reference-free guardrails plus an A/B test so the real KPI is the arbiter, not the offline proxy. You also re-check whether the 'better' prompt overfit to your eval examples.
fs: Diagnoses distribution gap/eval-score drift and feeds failures back into a stratified set plus online checks.
fj: Trusts the offline score as final and blames users instead of the eval coverage.

followups.q: How do you distinguish 'eval set was unrepresentative' from 'the prompt overfit the eval'?
followups.a: Test the new prompt on a fresh held-out sample of recent production traffic the prompt never saw. If it underperforms there too, the prompt overfit your eval examples. If it does fine on fresh traffic but the complaints cluster in a specific intent, the gate set simply lacked that cohort. The held-out fresh slice separates the two diagnoses.

followups.q: How do you decide which complaints are worth adding to the golden set?
followups.a: Prioritize by frequency and severity: cluster complaints by intent, weight by traffic volume and business impact, and add representative cases plus the worst edge cases, not every one-off. You want the set to stay lean and stratified, so you add cases that generalize to a recurring failure mode rather than bloating it with duplicates.

followups.q: Why add online guardrails if you're already fixing the offline set?
followups.a: Because the offline set can only cover failures you've already seen; online guardrails (faithfulness, toxicity, feedback) catch the next unanticipated distribution shift in real time before it spreads. Offline is reactive to known failures, online is your early-warning system for unknown ones. You need both layers, not just a patched golden set.

q: How do you size and stratify a golden eval set, and why isn't bigger always better?
a: A practical golden set is small and version-controlled, often 50-200 cases, stratified across the common user intents plus known edge cases and failure modes so the average isn't dominated by easy traffic. It must be large enough for statistical power on the effects you care about but small enough that humans can label it carefully with high agreement, since sloppy labels at scale just add noise. Bigger sets cost more to label and maintain and tempt you to over-trust an aggregate that hides per-slice regressions. You pair the lean deterministic golden gate with random production sampling to surface new failures.
fs: Calls for a stratified 50-200 case set balancing power against label quality, plus production sampling.
fj: Assumes a huge unlabeled or unstratified dump of examples is automatically a better eval set.

followups.q: How few cases per slice is too few for the per-slice gate to mean anything?
followups.a: Below roughly 20-30 per slice the per-slice estimate is too noisy to gate on; a single flip swings the rate wildly. You either invest to label more in critical slices, report those slices as directional-only, or merge thin slices. The 50-200 total has to be allocated so decision-critical slices clear a minimum, not spread uniformly.

followups.q: Bigger sets give more statistical power. Why is that not the dominant consideration?
followups.a: Power is wasted if labels are noisy: a large set labeled sloppily has low agreement, so its ground truth, and thus any judge validated against it, is unreliable. Label quality caps achievable measurement quality. A small, carefully-adjudicated, stratified set beats a big noisy dump; you buy power via variance reduction, not raw size.

followups.q: How does the random production sampling complement the curated gate?
followups.a: The curated gate is deterministic regression coverage for known cases; random production sampling surfaces unknown new failures and distribution shift the curated set can't anticipate. You label the sampled finds and graduate recurring ones into the golden set. Together: the gate prevents known regressions, sampling discovers what to add next.

q: Your judge gives a clean average score but your best-of-N selection keeps picking worse answers. What's happening?
a: Good aggregate judge metrics don't guarantee good per-item decisions: best-of-N depends on the judge's ranking being correct on each comparison, and a judge can have decent mean calibration while its fine-grained ordering is noisy or tied. Recent work shows judge scores that look fine in aggregate still fail budgeted best-of-N audits because small score gaps fall inside the judge's noise. The fixes are pairwise comparison instead of pointwise scoring for selection, requiring a minimum margin or explicit uncertainty/confidence-interval width before trusting a pick, and resampling to break ties. Validate the judge specifically on the selection task, not just on average agreement.
fs: Separates aggregate calibration from per-item ranking reliability and fixes selection with pairwise/margin/uncertainty.
fj: Assumes a good average judge score means good best-of-N decisions.

followups.q: Why does best-of-N amplify the judge's ranking noise rather than average it out?
followups.a: Best-of-N takes the argmax, which is a maximization over noisy scores, so it preferentially selects candidates whose noise happened to inflate them, an order-statistics effect that gets worse as N grows. Averaging cancels noise; argmax chases it. So a judge fine on means can systematically pick noise-favored losers, and larger N makes it worse, not better.

followups.q: Why does a minimum-margin requirement help, and what do you do on ties?
followups.a: Requiring the top candidate to beat the runner-up by more than the judge's noise band means you only act when the gap is real, not noise. Within the band, treat it as a tie and break it cheaply, by a secondary criterion, resampling more judge calls to tighten the estimate, or defaulting to the lower-cost/lower-latency candidate. The margin converts noisy argmax into a confident decision.

followups.q: How do you validate a judge specifically for selection rather than for scoring?
followups.a: Build a labeled set of candidate pairs/groups with known best answers and measure the judge's top-1 selection accuracy and pairwise win-rate agreement with humans, not just score correlation. A judge can have high score kappa yet poor selection accuracy. You audit the decision the judge will actually make, under your N and margin, before trusting it to pick.

q: How would you use an LLM judge to grade a classification task, and what metrics do you report?
a: When an LLM performs a labeling task (intent, sentiment, routing), you evaluate it like any classifier against a labeled set: report precision, recall, and F1 per class, plus a confusion matrix, not just overall accuracy, because class imbalance makes accuracy misleading. Pick the operating point by which error is costlier (recall when misses are dangerous, precision when false positives are expensive), and use macro-F1 to weight rare classes equally. If the LLM emits free-text labels you first normalize them to the label set before scoring. Track per-class metrics over time so a model upgrade can't silently wreck one rare but important class.
fs: Treats the LLM as a classifier with per-class precision/recall/F1 and confusion matrix, not bare accuracy.
fj: Reports a single accuracy number and ignores class imbalance and per-class behavior.

followups.q: Macro vs micro vs weighted F1 — when does the choice flip your conclusion?
followups.a: On imbalanced data they diverge: micro-F1 and weighted-F1 lean toward the majority class and can look great while a rare class fails, whereas macro-F1 averages classes equally and exposes a wrecked rare class. If the rare class is the important one (fraud, safety), report macro-F1 or that class's own recall, or you'll ship a model that's 'accurate' but blind where it matters.

followups.q: The LLM emits a label outside your allowed set. How do you score it?
followups.a: You normalize first, mapping near-misses to canonical labels, but track the off-vocabulary rate as its own quality signal rather than silently coercing everything. Truly unmappable outputs count as errors. Constrained decoding or an enum-typed output schema prevents most of this at the source, which is preferable to lossy post-hoc normalization.

followups.q: Why track per-class metrics over time instead of just at launch?
followups.a: Model and prompt upgrades drift, and an aggregate can hold steady while one rare class silently collapses, the kind of regression a single accuracy number hides. Per-class time series with alerts catch a class-specific drop the moment it happens. It's the classification analog of slice-level regression gating, protecting rare-but-critical categories.

q: How do you evaluate structured/JSON output from an LLM, and why isn't 'it parsed' enough?
a: Structured-output eval has layers: schema-validity rate (does it parse and conform to the JSON schema), field-level correctness against ground truth, and downstream task success once the data is consumed. 'It parsed' only proves syntactic validity, not that the values are right or that required fields are populated, so a syntactically valid but semantically wrong object can still trigger a bad refund or action. You measure each field's accuracy, track the validity rate as a guardrail, and for action-triggering outputs treat schema compliance as a hard requirement enforced by constrained decoding. Report where failures land: malformed, valid-but-wrong-field, or valid-but-incomplete.
fs: Separates schema-validity from field-level correctness and downstream success, treating compliance as a hard gate.
fj: Considers the eval passed as soon as the JSON parses, ignoring field correctness.

followups.q: If constrained decoding guarantees valid JSON, can you stop measuring validity?
followups.a: You can stop worrying about parse failures but not about correctness: constrained decoding forces the shape, not the values, so wrong-but-valid fields and hallucinated enum choices still slip through. It can also degrade quality by over-constraining generation. Keep field-level accuracy and downstream-success metrics; validity just stops being the bottleneck.

followups.q: Naive field equality fails on dates and free-text fields. How do you score those?
followups.a: Use per-field comparators: exact for IDs and enums, normalized equality for dates/numbers (parse then compare), and semantic similarity or an LLM judge for free-text fields where many surface forms are correct. A flat `out[k]==gold[k]` punishes valid formatting variation. Match the comparator to each field's type, and report accuracy per field.

followups.q: Why report the failure category (malformed vs wrong-field vs incomplete) instead of one pass rate?
followups.a: Each category points at a different fix: malformed means add constrained decoding, wrong-field means improve the prompt or retrieval, incomplete means tighten required-field instructions. A single pass rate tells you it broke but not where to look. The breakdown turns the metric into an actionable diagnosis, and each failure mode has a different blast radius for action-triggering outputs.

q: Walk me through running a red-team / safety evaluation on an LLM product. What do you measure?
a: You assemble adversarial probes across harm categories (jailbreaks, prompt injection, PII leakage, toxic or biased output, unsafe tool actions) and run them through the system, measuring attack success rate per category and overall. Modern practice uses automated red-team agents that generate and mutate attacks (roleplay, crescendo, encoding tricks), which reach high success rates and scale far past hand-written prompts. You report ASR by attack type, severity beyond a binary pass/fail, and time-to-jailbreak, then track these as guardrail metrics that must not regress across model and prompt upgrades. Red-teaming is continuous and gated in the deploy pipeline, not a one-time audit.
fs: Frames safety eval as per-category attack-success-rate from automated adversarial agents, run continuously.
fj: Thinks a handful of manual 'try to break it' prompts once is sufficient safety coverage.

followups.q: Why report attack-success-rate by category and severity instead of one overall pass/fail?
followups.a: Harms aren't fungible: a 5% ASR on benign-policy refusals is tolerable, but any non-trivial ASR on PII leakage or unsafe tool actions is a hard block. A single overall rate averages a catastrophic category against minor ones and hides the dangerous one. Per-category, severity-weighted reporting lets you set different thresholds per harm and gate on the worst.

followups.q: Why does agentic tool use widen the red-team surface beyond text generation?
followups.a: An agent can take real actions, so prompt injection in a retrieved document or tool result can hijack it into exfiltrating data, calling a destructive tool, or escalating, harms a chat-only model can't cause. You must red-team the tool layer (argument injection, confused-deputy, untrusted-content-as-instructions), not just the text output. The blast radius is actions, not words.

followups.q: Automated red-team agents find ever more attacks. When are you 'done'?
followups.a: You're never done; it's continuous because new jailbreak techniques and model updates constantly shift the surface. 'Done enough to ship' means ASR is below your per-category thresholds on the current attack suite, with the suite itself versioned and growing. You gate each release against it and treat residual ASR as a tracked, owned risk, not a solved problem.

q: What is noise sensitivity in RAG evaluation and why does it matter beyond faithfulness?
a: Noise sensitivity measures how much answer quality degrades when irrelevant or distractor chunks are retrieved alongside the relevant ones, capturing whether the generator gets misled by junk in its context. It matters because a system can score well on faithfulness and recall in clean conditions yet fall apart in production where retrieval inevitably returns some noise. High noise sensitivity points you at the generator (or at tightening retrieval/reranking to cut distractors), distinct from a pure recall miss. Tracking it stops you from shipping a pipeline that only works when retrieval is perfect.
fs: Defines noise sensitivity as quality degradation under distractor chunks and ties it to generator robustness.
fj: Assumes good faithfulness on clean context means the system is robust to retrieval noise.

followups.q: How do you actually construct a test to measure noise sensitivity?
followups.a: Take cases with known relevant chunks and deliberately inject controlled distractors (hard negatives, near-duplicate-but-wrong, or off-topic passages) at varying ratios, then measure how faithfulness/correctness drops as noise rises. The slope is your sensitivity. Hard negatives that are topically close but factually wrong stress the generator far more than random junk.

followups.q: If a system is highly noise-sensitive, do you fix the generator or the retriever?
followups.a: Both are valid levers: tighten retrieval/reranking to cut distractors (reduce the noise) or make the generator more robust via prompting to weigh evidence and ignore unsupported context (tolerate the noise). Pick by cost: reranking is often the cheaper, more reliable fix, while generator robustness helps when some noise is unavoidable. Usually you do both.

followups.q: Why can't faithfulness alone reveal a noise-sensitivity problem?
followups.a: Faithfulness on clean context only tests the best case; a noise-sensitive generator can be perfectly faithful when retrieval is clean yet latch onto a distractor when it isn't. The failure only appears under noise, which clean-context faithfulness never exercises. You need to deliberately inject distractors to surface it, otherwise you ship something that works only when retrieval is perfect.

q: Context entities recall and answer correctness in RAGAS — what do they add over the core four metrics?
a: Context entities recall checks the fraction of ground-truth entities (names, dates, IDs) present in the retrieved context, a sharper retrieval signal for fact-heavy queries where missing one entity sinks the answer even if overall recall looks fine. Answer correctness combines factual similarity to the reference with semantic similarity, giving a single reference-based 'is the final answer right' score that faithfulness and relevancy alone don't provide, since those measure grounding and on-topic-ness, not truth. Together they cover the gaps: entities recall localizes a specific retrieval miss, correctness gates end-to-end truth against ground truth. Both need labeled references, so they're offline metrics.
fs: Maps entities recall to fact-level retrieval misses and answer correctness to reference-based end-to-end truth.
fj: Treats faithfulness and relevancy as already covering whether the final answer is correct.

followups.q: When does context entities recall add signal that plain context recall misses?
followups.a: On fact-heavy queries where one missing entity (a specific date, ID, or name) breaks the answer but barely moves chunk-level recall. Standard recall can read high because most relevant chunks came back, masking the single critical-entity miss. Entities recall zooms to the atomic facts that actually determine correctness, useful for lookups, finance, and compliance queries.

followups.q: Answer correctness blends factual and semantic similarity. What's the risk in that blend?
followups.a: The semantic-similarity component can reward a fluent answer that's topically close to the reference but factually wrong, partly masking a hard factual error behind a high blended score. So a high correctness score isn't proof of truth on critical facts. For high-stakes outputs, weight or separate the factual component, or add a claim-level check, rather than trusting the blended number.

followups.q: Both need labels. How do you get reference entities and answers at scale affordably?
followups.a: You curate a stratified offline set rather than labeling everything: humans author references for a representative slice, optionally bootstrapped by an LLM extracting candidate entities/answers that a human verifies. These are offline-only gates by design. Online you fall back to reference-free proxies (faithfulness, entity presence in output) since live traffic has no ground truth.

q: An agent calls every required tool correctly but still produces an answer a domain expert would reject. How does your eval catch this?
a: Tool-call accuracy is necessary but not sufficient: an agent can hit 100% on tool selection and arguments yet violate policy or synthesize the results poorly. So you evaluate at three levels — task success (did the end goal get met to expert standard), trajectory quality (tool accuracy, step efficiency, progress rate toward the goal), and a final-output quality judge against a rubric or expert-labeled gold. The expert-rejection case is caught by the outcome and output-quality layers, not the trajectory layer, which is exactly why you can't stop at tool metrics. You also slice failures to localize whether planning, a specific tool, or synthesis broke.
fs: Adds outcome and output-quality layers above tool-call accuracy to catch correct-tools-but-bad-answer.
fj: Declares the agent good because tool-call accuracy is 100%.

followups.q: How do you localize whether the failure was planning, a tool, or synthesis?
followups.a: Instrument per-step: check tool selection/arguments (planning), each tool's returned output against expectation (tool layer), and whether the final answer correctly uses correct tool outputs (synthesis). If tools fired right and returned right data but the answer is wrong, it's a synthesis failure. Step-level traces turn a single bad outcome into a pinpointed component bug.

followups.q: Why is an expert-labeled output rubric needed here rather than a generic judge?
followups.a: Domain rejection often hinges on policy, regulatory, or specialist correctness a generic judge doesn't encode, so it would pass a plausible-sounding but expert-wrong answer. An expert-derived rubric (or expert-labeled gold to validate the judge against) captures those domain constraints. You're encoding the expert's acceptance criteria into the metric, not asking a model for vibes.

followups.q: Tool-call accuracy can be 100% yet the agent still violates policy. How does that happen?
followups.a: The agent can call all the right tools and still synthesize their outputs into a policy-violating recommendation, or take a technically-valid action that's disallowed in context (e.g. issuing a refund the policy forbids for that case). Tool accuracy measures mechanics, not judgment. The outcome/policy layer, ideally a hard rule check plus expert rubric, catches the violation tool metrics miss.

q: What is progress rate as an agent metric, and why prefer it over a binary task-success flag?
a: Progress rate compares the agent's actual trajectory against the expected one to measure how far toward the goal it got, rather than a single pass/fail at the end. It's more informative because most agent runs partially succeed, and a binary flag throws away the signal that distinguishes 'reached step 4 of 5 then looped' from 'failed immediately', which matters for debugging and for detecting small regressions. On long multi-step tasks where end-to-end success is rare and noisy, progress rate gives a denser, lower-variance metric to optimize and to gate on. You still keep task success as the headline, but progress rate tells you where it's breaking.
fs: Defines progress rate as fractional advance vs expected path and values its denser signal over binary success.
fj: Only logs whether the whole task passed, discarding partial-progress information.

followups.q: Optimizing progress rate can be gamed. What's the perverse incentive?
followups.a: An agent (or your tuning) can learn to rack up sub-goal credit without finishing, taking many partially-correct steps that score progress but never close the task, the reward-hacking trap of dense proxies. You guard it by keeping task success as the headline gate and treating progress rate as diagnostic only, plus penalizing wasted/looping steps so progress without completion isn't rewarded.

followups.q: Defining 'expected trajectory' is hard when multiple valid paths exist. How do you compute progress then?
followups.a: Anchor progress to sub-goals/milestones rather than a specific step sequence: define the state-changes that constitute advancement (auth done, record fetched, action taken) and credit any path that achieves them. This is path-agnostic and tolerates alternative routes, unlike step-by-step match. You measure how many required milestones were reached, not how closely one canonical path was followed.

followups.q: Why is progress rate lower-variance than binary success on long tasks?
followups.a: Binary success is a single Bernoulli outcome that's mostly 0 on hard long tasks, so its estimate is noisy and needs many runs to move. Progress rate is a graded fraction that captures partial advances, giving more bits of information per run and a denser gradient. That lower variance means you detect small regressions and tune with far fewer samples.

q: Frontier providers silently change model behavior even on pinned IDs. How do you keep your evals trustworthy across that drift?
a: Treat the model as a moving target: even version-stamped IDs have shifted behavior between dates, so you schedule your reference golden set to re-run on a cadence (and on every provider update) and alert on any score degradation as a drift signal. You separate prompt drift, model drift, and eval-score drift so you know whether the input, the model, or your metric moved.
