Pin model versions where the provider allows it, log model IDs with every eval run for attribution, and re-validate your LLM judge after upgrades since the judge itself drifts. The curated reference dataset run on a schedule is the gold-standard drift detector.
fs: Schedules golden-set reruns as a drift detector, distinguishes prompt/model/eval drift, and re-validates the judge.
fj: Assumes a pinned model ID is frozen, so evals never need re-running until you change code.

followups.q: If the judge also drifts, how do you avoid a drift alert that's really just judge drift?
followups.a: Disentangle them: keep a small human-labeled anchor set and re-validate the judge against it on the same cadence, so you know whether a score drop reflects the system or your measuring instrument. If judge-vs-human agreement moved, it's judge drift; if the judge still agrees with humans but system scores dropped, it's real. Without anchoring the judge, you can't tell.

followups.q: How do you separate prompt drift, model drift, and eval-score drift in one alert?
followups.a: Hold two of the three fixed and vary one: version-control prompts, pin and log model IDs per run, and validate the metric against a fixed anchor. A score move with unchanged prompt and metric implicates the model; a move right after a prompt edit implicates the prompt; a move with both fixed implicates the metric. Logging all three per run makes attribution mechanical.

followups.q: A pinned snapshot eventually gets deprecated. How do you handle the forced migration?
followups.a: Treat it as a planned model change: run the golden set on the successor before cutover to quantify the behavior delta, re-validate the judge on it, and diff per-slice so you catch regressions a single aggregate hides. Migrate behind an A/B or canary on the real KPI rather than swapping blind. Pinning buys stability, but you plan the inevitable migration as a gated change, not a surprise.

q: How do you treat cost and latency as first-class evaluation dimensions rather than afterthoughts?
a: Quality isn't free, so you score every candidate on cost-per-query (tokens in/out times price) and latency (p50/p95), and gate rollout on these as guardrails alongside the quality metric. A change that lifts judge score but doubles p95 latency or token cost may be a net loss in production, especially for agents where extra steps multiply both. You plot a quality-vs-cost frontier to pick the operating point instead of chasing maximum quality blindly, and you set hard latency/cost ceilings so a verbose 'better' answer can't silently blow the budget. Reporting them per change keeps the trade-off explicit in ship decisions.
fs: Makes cost-per-query and p95 latency guardrails and reasons on a quality-vs-cost frontier.
fj: Optimizes only for quality scores and ignores token cost and latency until production bills arrive.

followups.q: Why gate on p95 latency rather than mean?
followups.a: Mean hides the tail, and it's the slow tail that breaks user experience, trips timeouts, and triggers retries that compound load. A change can leave the mean flat while fattening p95/p99, the requests that actually hurt. Tail percentiles capture worst-case behavior users feel, so you gate on p95 (and watch p99), not the average that smooths it away.

followups.q: What does the quality-vs-cost frontier let you decide that a single quality number can't?
followups.a: It exposes the trade-off so you can pick the operating point with the best quality per dollar/ms rather than the absolute max quality. Two candidates with equal quality but very different cost are obviously distinguishable on the frontier but identical on a quality-only metric. It also reveals diminishing returns, where extra spend buys negligible quality, so you stop over-paying.

followups.q: Why do these dimensions matter even more for agents than single-call apps?
followups.a: Agents take many steps, and cost and latency are roughly multiplicative in step count, so a small per-step regression or one extra tool call compounds into large bill and tail-latency swings. A change that adds a verification step can dominate the budget. That's why step efficiency is itself an agent metric, and you gate cost/latency tightly on trajectory length.

q: How do you keep LLM A/B tests from needing impractically huge sample sizes given how noisy the metrics are?
a: LLM metrics like judge scores are stochastic and heavy-tailed, which inflates variance and therefore the sample size needed to detect a given effect, so the lever is reducing per-unit variance. Use within-subject/paired designs (same inputs through both variants) and variance-reduction like CUPED that regresses out pre-experiment covariates, which cuts required samples without biasing the estimate. Use deterministic decoding where the product allows it to remove sampling noise, and pick a metric that's less skewed (pass-rate over a raw 1-5 mean). You still run a power analysis from baseline, MDE, and the reduced variance to set the actual sample size.
fs: Attacks required-N by reducing variance (paired design, CUPED, deterministic decoding) before powering the test.
fj: Just collects more and more samples without addressing the variance driving the requirement.

followups.q: Deterministic decoding cuts variance but changes the product. What's the catch?
followups.a: Temperature 0 measures a system users won't actually experience if production samples at higher temperature, so your low-variance estimate can mismatch live behavior. It also isn't perfectly deterministic at the provider level. Use it for offline tuning and regression gates, but for the user-facing A/B you must evaluate the real sampling configuration, accepting the higher variance.

followups.q: Why does pass-rate over a threshold need fewer samples than a raw 1-5 judge mean?
followups.a: The raw mean's variance is inflated by heavy tails and the judge's fine-grained noise, which a thresholded pass/fail collapses into a stabler Bernoulli signal less sensitive to extreme scores. You trade some resolution for much lower variance, and a well-chosen threshold aligned to the decision often loses little real information. Lower variance directly shrinks required N.

followups.q: When does CUPED stop helping?
followups.a: Its variance reduction is proportional to how strongly the pre-experiment covariate correlates with the outcome; for a brand-new feature or new users with no informative history, the correlation is weak and CUPED barely moves N. It also can't fix variance from the treatment effect itself. When pre-period signal is thin, you fall back to paired designs, less-skewed metrics, or simply more data.

q: When validating an LLM judge against human labels, what agreement target is reasonable and what do you do if you fall short?
a: A common practical bar is having domain experts label a representative sample (often around 200 cases) and iterating the judge prompt until it reaches roughly 85-90% agreement with experts, measured with a chance-corrected coefficient like kappa, not raw percent. If you fall short, the usual culprit is an underspecified rubric, so you tighten criteria, add worked positive/negative examples, and decompose the judgment (QAG/decision-tree style) before blaming the model. If agreement still won't move, your humans may not agree either, which caps any achievable judge quality and sends you back to fix the rubric and adjudicate labels. Re-measure after every judge-prompt change.
fs: Cites an ~85-90% expert-agreement target via kappa and treats shortfalls as rubric/decomposition problems.
fj: Accepts the judge with no agreement target, or reads raw percent agreement as good enough.

followups.q: What does decomposing the judgment (QAG / decision-tree) actually buy you over a single 'rate it' prompt?
followups.a: Breaking the verdict into specific yes/no sub-questions (is each claim supported? on-topic? any policy violation?) turns a fuzzy holistic rating into concrete checks the judge and humans both answer more consistently, which raises agreement and makes disagreements traceable to one criterion. A single 'rate 1-10' bundles everything into one noisy number with no traceability.

followups.q: Your human inter-annotator agreement is only 0.6. What does an 85% judge target even mean then?
followups.a: It's incoherent to chase 85% judge-human agreement when humans agree only at 0.6, that human ceiling caps any achievable judge quality, since there's no stable ground truth to match. You fix the humans first: tighten the rubric, adjudicate disagreements, simplify the scale, or accept the task is subjective and move to pairwise. The judge can't be more reliable than the labels validating it.

followups.q: Why re-measure agreement after every judge-prompt change rather than once?
followups.a: A prompt edit is a change to your measuring instrument; even a small wording tweak can shift the judge's biases and recalibrate its scores, silently invalidating prior validation. Treat each judge-prompt version like a code deploy that needs its own test against the gold set. Logging the judge-prompt version per run lets you attribute any score shift to the instrument versus the system.

q: What's the difference between using an LLM judge as an offline scorer versus an online guardrail, and what changes operationally?
a: Offline, the judge scores a fixed golden set in CI where latency and cost barely matter, so you can use a slow, expensive frontier judge with chain-of-thought and pairwise comparisons to gate merges. Online, the same judging logic runs on live traffic in the request path or async monitoring, where its own latency, cost, and failure modes become production concerns, pushing you to a cheaper/faster judge or sampling rather than judging every call. Online judges also need fallback behavior (fail-open vs fail-closed) and can't rely on ground truth, so they lean on reference-free checks like faithfulness or toxicity. You validate both against humans, but the online one trades some accuracy for speed and resilience.
fs: Contrasts a heavy offline CI judge with a cheap, sampled, fail-safe online judge under latency/cost constraints.
fj: Assumes you can run the same expensive offline judge on every production request for free.

followups.q: Fail-open vs fail-closed when the online judge errors or times out — how do you choose?
followups.a: By the cost of each error: fail-closed (block the response) for safety-critical checks where letting harmful output through is worse than a degraded UX; fail-open (serve it, log for async review) for soft-quality checks where blocking a probably-fine answer hurts more. Set it per check by harm severity, and alert on elevated failure rates so a silently-degraded guardrail stays visible.

followups.q: If you only sample a fraction of online traffic for judging, what do you risk missing?
followups.a: Rare but severe failures that fall between samples, so sampling is fine for trend monitoring but unsafe as the sole guard on high-severity harms. You stratify: judge 100% on cheap deterministic safety checks (regex/classifier) and sample the expensive LLM judge for quality trends. Critical categories get full coverage with a cheap detector; the LLM judge samples for the nuanced stuff.

followups.q: Does an inline judge in the request path defeat the purpose by adding the very latency you guard?
followups.a: Often yes, which is why most quality judging runs async out of the band, scoring responses after they're served for monitoring and feedback, not gating. You reserve inline (synchronous) judging for hard safety blocks where you must not serve the output, and keep those checks cheap/fast. Putting a slow CoT judge in the hot path trades one production problem for another.
